1→defmodule Hermes.Transport.StreamableHTTP do
     2→  @moduledoc """
     3→  A transport implementation that uses Streamable HTTP as specified in MCP 2025-03-26.
     4→
     5→  This transport communicates with MCP servers via HTTP POST requests for sending messages
     6→  and optionally uses Server-Sent Events (SSE) for receiving streaming responses.
     7→
     8→  ## Usage
     9→
    10→      # Start the transport with a base URL
    11→      {:ok, transport} = Hermes.Transport.StreamableHTTP.start_link(
    12→        client: client_pid,
    13→        base_url: "http://localhost:8000",
    14→        mcp_path: "/mcp"
    15→      )
    16→
    17→      # Send a message
    18→      :ok = Hermes.Transport.StreamableHTTP.send_message(transport, encoded_message)
    19→
    20→  ## Session Management
    21→
    22→  The transport automatically handles MCP session IDs via the `mcp-session-id` header:
    23→  - Extracts session ID from server responses
    24→  - Includes session ID in subsequent requests
    25→  - Maintains session state throughout the connection lifecycle
    26→  - Handles session expiration (404 responses) by reinitializing
    27→
    28→  ## Response Handling
    29→
    30→  Based on the response status and content type:
    31→  - 202 Accepted: Message acknowledged, no immediate response
    32→  - 200 OK with application/json: Single JSON response forwarded to client
    33→  - 200 OK with text/event-stream: SSE stream parsed and events forwarded to client
    34→  - 404 Not Found: Session expired, triggers reinitialization
    35→
    36→  ## SSE Support
    37→
    38→  The transport can establish a separate GET connection for server-initiated messages.
    39→  This allows the server to send requests and notifications without a client request.
    40→  """
    41→
    42→  @behaviour Hermes.Transport.Behaviour
    43→
    44→  use GenServer
    45→  use Hermes.Logging
    46→
    47→  import Peri
    48→
    49→  alias Hermes.HTTP
    50→  alias Hermes.SSE
    51→  alias Hermes.SSE.Event
    52→  alias Hermes.Telemetry
    53→  alias Hermes.Transport.Behaviour, as: Transport
    54→
    55→  @default_timeout 15_000
    56→
    57→  @type t :: GenServer.server()
    58→  @type params_t :: Enumerable.t(option)
    59→
    60→  @typedoc """
    61→  The options for the Streamable HTTP transport.
    62→
    63→  - `:base_url` - The base URL of the MCP server (e.g. http://localhost:8000) (required).
    64→  - `:mcp_path` - The MCP endpoint path (e.g. /mcp) (default "/mcp").
    65→  - `:client` - The client to send the messages to.
    66→  - `:headers` - The headers to send with the HTTP requests.
    67→  - `:transport_opts` - The underlying HTTP transport options.
    68→  - `:http_options` - The underlying HTTP client options.
    69→  - `:enable_sse` - Whether to establish a GET connection for server-initiated messages (default false).
    70→  """
    71→  @type option ::
    72→          {:name, GenServer.name()}
    73→          | {:client, GenServer.server()}
    74→          | {:base_url, String.t()}
    75→          | {:mcp_path, String.t()}
    76→          | {:headers, map()}
    77→          | {:transport_opts, keyword}
    78→          | {:http_options, Finch.request_opts()}
    79→          | {:enable_sse, boolean()}
    80→          | GenServer.option()
    81→
    82→  defschema(:options_schema, %{
    83→    name: {{:custom, &Hermes.genserver_name/1}, {:default, __MODULE__}},
    84→    client: {:required, Hermes.get_schema(:process_name)},
    85→    base_url: {:required, {:string, {:transform, &URI.new!/1}}},
    86→    mcp_path: {:string, {:default, "/"}},
    87→    headers: {:map, {:default, %{}}},
    88→    transport_opts: {:any, {:default, []}},
    89→    http_options: {:any, {:default, []}},
    90→    enable_sse: {:boolean, {:default, false}}
    91→  })
    92→
    93→  @impl Transport
    94→  @spec start_link(params_t) :: GenServer.on_start()
    95→  def start_link(opts \\ []) do
    96→    opts = options_schema!(opts)
    97→    GenServer.start_link(__MODULE__, Map.new(opts), name: opts[:name])
    98→  end
    99→
   100→  @impl Transport
   101→  def send_message(pid \\ __MODULE__, message, opts \\ []) when is_binary(message) do
   102→    timeout = Keyword.get(opts, :timeout, @default_timeout)
   103→    GenServer.call(pid, {:send, message, opts}, timeout)
   104→  end
   105→
   106→  @impl Transport
   107→  def shutdown(pid \\ __MODULE__) do
   108→    GenServer.cast(pid, :close_connection)
   109→  end
   110→
   111→  @impl Transport
   112→  def supported_protocol_versions, do: ["2025-03-26", "2025-06-18"]
   113→
   114→  @impl GenServer
   115→  def init(opts) do
   116→    state = %{
   117→      client: opts.client,
   118→      mcp_url: URI.append_path(opts.base_url, opts.mcp_path),
   119→      headers: opts.headers,
   120→      transport_opts: opts.transport_opts,
   121→      http_options: opts.http_options,
   122→      session_id: nil,
   123→      enable_sse: Map.get(opts, :enable_sse, false),
   124→      sse_task: nil,
   125→      last_event_id: nil,
   126→      active_request: nil
   127→    }
   128→
   129→    emit_telemetry(:init, state)
   130→    {:ok, state, {:continue, :connect}}
   131→  end
   132→
   133→  @impl GenServer
   134→  def handle_continue(:connect, state) do
   135→    emit_telemetry(:connect, state)
   136→    GenServer.cast(state.client, :initialize)
   137→    new_state = maybe_start_sse_connection(state)
   138→    {:noreply, new_state}
   139→  end
   140→
   141→  @impl GenServer
   142→  def handle_call({:send, message, opts}, from, state) do
   143→    emit_telemetry(:send, state, %{message_size: byte_size(message)})
   144→
   145→    headers = Keyword.get(opts, :headers)
   146→    log_event = if headers, do: "sending_http_request_with_headers", else: "sending_http_request"
   147→
   148→    log_data = %{
   149→      url: URI.to_string(state.mcp_url),
   150→      size: byte_size(message)
   151→    }
   152→
   153→    log_data = if headers, do: Map.put(log_data, :additional_headers, Map.keys(headers)), else: log_data
   154→
   155→    Logging.transport_event(log_event, log_data)
   156→
   157→    new_state = %{state | active_request: from}
   158→
   159→    case send_http_request(new_state, message, headers) do
   160→      {:ok, response} ->
   161→        Logging.transport_event("got_http_response", %{status: response.status})
   162→        handle_response(response, new_state)
   163→
   164→      {:error, {:http_error, 404, _body}} when not is_nil(state.session_id) ->
   165→        Logging.transport_event("session_expired", %{session_id: state.session_id})
   166→        {:reply, {:error, :session_expired}, %{new_state | session_id: nil}}
   167→
   168→      {:error, reason} ->
   169→        Logging.transport_event("http_request_error", %{reason: inspect(reason)}, level: :error)
   170→        log_error(reason)
   171→        {:reply, {:error, reason}, %{new_state | active_request: nil}}
   172→    end
   173→  end
   174→
   175→  @impl GenServer
   176→  def handle_cast(:close_connection, state) do
   177→    if state.session_id, do: delete_session(state)
   178→    if state.sse_task, do: Task.shutdown(state.sse_task, :brutal_kill)
   179→
   180→    {:stop, :normal, state}
   181→  end
   182→
   183→  @impl GenServer
   184→  def handle_info({:sse_event, event}, state) do
   185→    handle_sse_event(event, state)
   186→    {:noreply, state}
   187→  end
   188→
   189→  @impl GenServer
   190→  def handle_info({:sse_response_event, event}, state) do
   191→    handle_sse_event(event, state)
   192→    {:noreply, state}
   193→  end
   194→
   195→  @impl GenServer
   196→  def handle_info(:sse_response_complete, state) do
   197→    if state.active_request, do: GenServer.reply(state.active_request, :ok)
   198→    {:noreply, %{state | active_request: nil}}
   199→  end
   200→
   201→  @impl GenServer
   202→  def handle_info({:sse_closed, reason}, state) do
   203→    Logging.transport_event("sse_connection_closed", %{reason: reason})
   204→    new_state = maybe_start_sse_connection(%{state | sse_task: nil})
   205→    {:noreply, new_state}
   206→  end
   207→
   208→  @impl GenServer
   209→  def handle_info({:DOWN, _ref, :process, pid, reason}, state) when state.sse_task != nil do
   210→    if pid == state.sse_task.pid do
   211→      Logging.transport_event("sse_task_down", %{reason: reason})
   212→      new_state = maybe_start_sse_connection(%{state | sse_task: nil})
   213→      {:noreply, new_state}
   214→    else
   215→      {:noreply, state}
   216→    end
   217→  end
   218→
   219→  @impl GenServer
   220→  def handle_info(msg, state) do
   221→    Logging.transport_event("unexpected_message", %{message: msg})
   222→    {:noreply, state}
   223→  end
   224→
   225→  @impl GenServer
   226→  def terminate(reason, state) do
   227→    if state.sse_task, do: Task.shutdown(state.sse_task, 5000)
   228→    if state.session_id, do: delete_session(state)
   229→
   230→    emit_telemetry(:terminate, state, %{reason: reason})
   231→  end
   232→
   233→  # Private functions
   234→
   235→  defp send_http_request(state, message, additional_headers) do
   236→    base_headers =
   237→      state.headers
   238→      |> Map.put("accept", "application/json, text/event-stream")
   239→      |> Map.put("content-type", "application/json")
   240→      |> put_session_header(state.session_id)
   241→
   242→    case additional_headers do
   243→      nil ->
   244→        do_send_http_request(state, message, base_headers)
   245→
   246→      headers when is_map(headers) ->
   247→        case validate_headers(headers) do
   248→          :ok ->
   249→            final_headers = Map.merge(base_headers, headers)
   250→            do_send_http_request(state, message, final_headers)
   251→
   252→          {:error, reason} ->
   253→            {:error, reason}
   254→        end
   255→    end
   256→  end
   257→
   258→  # Common HTTP request logic
   259→  defp do_send_http_request(state, message, headers) do
   260→    options = [transport_opts: state.transport_opts] ++ state.http_options
   261→    url = URI.to_string(state.mcp_url)
   262→
   263→    Logging.transport_event("http_request", %{
   264→      method: :post,
   265→      url: url,
   266→      headers: headers
   267→    })
   268→
   269→    request = HTTP.build(:post, url, headers, message, options)
   270→
   271→    request
   272→    |> HTTP.follow_redirect()
   273→    |> case do
   274→      {:ok, %{status: status} = response} when status in 200..299 ->
   275→        {:ok, response}
   276→
   277→      {:ok, %{status: status, body: body}} ->
   278→        {:error, {:http_error, status, body}}
   279→
   280→      {:error, reason} = error ->
   281→        Logging.transport_event("http_request_failed", %{reason: reason}, level: :error)
   282→        error
   283→    end
   284→  end
   285→
   286→  defp handle_response(%{headers: headers, body: body, status: status}, state) do
   287→    new_state = update_session_id(state, headers)
   288→
   289→    Logging.transport_event("http_response", %{
   290→      status: status,
   291→      content_type: get_content_type(headers),
   292→      body_size: byte_size(body),
   293→      has_session: not is_nil(new_state.session_id)
   294→    })
   295→
   296→    case {status, get_content_type(headers)} do
   297→      {202, _} ->
   298→        {:reply, :ok, %{new_state | active_request: nil}}
   299→
   300→      {_, "application/json"} ->
   301→        forward_to_client(body, new_state)
   302→        {:reply, :ok, %{new_state | active_request: nil}}
   303→
   304→      {_, "text/event-stream"} ->
   305→        stream_sse_response(body, new_state)
   306→        {:noreply, new_state}
   307→
   308→      {_, content_type} ->
   309→        {:reply, {:error, {:unsupported_content_type, content_type}}, %{new_state | active_request: nil}}
   310→    end
   311→  end
   312→
   313→  defp forward_to_client(message, %{client: client} = state) do
   314→    emit_telemetry(:receive, state, %{message_size: byte_size(message)})
   315→    GenServer.cast(client, {:response, message})
   316→  end
   317→
   318→  defp stream_sse_response(body, state) do
   319→    parent = self()
   320→
   321→    Task.start(fn ->
   322→      body
   323→      |> SSE.Parser.run()
   324→      |> Enum.each(fn event ->
   325→        send(parent, {:sse_response_event, event})
   326→      end)
   327→
   328→      send(parent, :sse_response_complete)
   329→    end)
   330→  rescue
   331→    e ->
   332→      Logging.transport_event("sse_parse_error", %{error: e}, level: :warning)
   333→
   334→      if state.active_request do
   335→        GenServer.reply(state.active_request, {:error, :sse_parse_error})
   336→      end
   337→  end
   338→
   339→  defp handle_sse_event({:error, :halted}, _state) do
   340→    Logging.transport_event("sse_halted", "SSE stream ended")
   341→  end
   342→
   343→  defp handle_sse_event(%Event{data: data, id: id}, state) do
   344→    new_state = if id, do: %{state | last_event_id: id}, else: state
   345→    forward_to_client(data, new_state)
   346→  end
   347→
   348→  defp handle_sse_event(event, _state) do
   349→    Logging.transport_event("unknown_sse_event", event, level: :warning)
   350→  end
   351→
   352→  # Header validation - fail fast with clear error messages
   353→  defp validate_headers(headers) when is_map(headers) do
   354→    case find_invalid_header(headers) do
   355→      nil ->
   356→        :ok
   357→
   358→      {key, value} ->
   359→        {:error, "Invalid header value for '#{key}': HTTP headers must be strings, got #{inspect(value)}"}
   360→    end
   361→  end
   362→
   363→  defp find_invalid_header(headers) do
   364→    Enum.find(headers, fn {_key, value} ->
   365→      not is_binary(value) and not is_nil(value)
   366→    end)
   367→  end
   368→
   369→  defp put_session_header(headers, nil), do: headers
   370→
   371→  defp put_session_header(headers, session_id), do: Map.put(headers, "mcp-session-id", session_id)
   372→
   373→  defp update_session_id(state, headers) do
   374→    case get_header(headers, "mcp-session-id") do
   375→      nil -> state
   376→      session_id -> %{state | session_id: session_id}
   377→    end
   378→  end
   379→
   380→  defp get_header(headers, name) do
   381→    headers
   382→    |> Enum.find(fn {key, _} -> String.downcase(key) == String.downcase(name) end)
   383→    |> case do
   384→      {_, value} -> value
   385→      nil -> nil
   386→    end
   387→  end
   388→
   389→  defp get_content_type(headers) do
   390→    headers
   391→    |> get_header("content-type")
   392→    |> case do
   393→      nil -> "application/json"
   394→      value -> value |> String.split(";") |> List.first() |> String.trim()
   395→    end
   396→  end
   397→
   398→  defp emit_telemetry(event, state, extra_metadata \\ %{}) do
   399→    metadata = %{
   400→      transport: :streamable_http,
   401→      mcp_url: URI.to_string(state.mcp_url),
   402→      client: state.client
   403→    }
   404→
   405→    event_name =
   406→      case event do
   407→        :init -> Telemetry.event_transport_init()
   408→        :connect -> Telemetry.event_transport_connect()
   409→        :send -> Telemetry.event_transport_send()
   410→        :receive -> Telemetry.event_transport_receive()
   411→        :terminate -> Telemetry.event_transport_terminate()
   412→      end
   413→
   414→    Telemetry.execute(
   415→      event_name,
   416→      %{system_time: System.system_time()},
   417→      Map.merge(metadata, extra_metadata)
   418→    )
   419→  end
   420→
   421→  defp log_error({:http_error, status, body}) do
   422→    Logging.transport_event("http_error", %{status: status, body: body}, level: :error)
   423→  end
   424→
   425→  defp log_error(reason) do
   426→    Logging.transport_event("request_error", %{reason: reason}, level: :error)
   427→  end
   428→
   429→  # Additional helper functions for SSE support
   430→
   431→  defp maybe_start_sse_connection(%{enable_sse: false} = state), do: state
   432→
   433→  defp maybe_start_sse_connection(%{enable_sse: true, session_id: nil} = state), do: state
   434→
   435→  defp maybe_start_sse_connection(%{enable_sse: true} = state) do
   436→    task = start_sse_task(state)
   437→    %{state | sse_task: task}
   438→  end
   439→
   440→  defp start_sse_task(state) do
   441→    parent = self()
   442→    Task.start_link(fn -> run_sse_task(parent, state) end)
   443→  end
   444→
   445→  defp run_sse_task(parent, state) do
   446→    headers = build_sse_headers(state)
   447→    options = [transport_opts: state.transport_opts] ++ state.http_options
   448→    request = HTTP.build(:get, URI.to_string(state.mcp_url), headers, nil, options)
   449→
   450→    process_sse_request(request, parent)
   451→    send(parent, {:sse_closed, :normal})
   452→  end
   453→
   454→  defp build_sse_headers(state) do
   455→    %{"accept" => "text/event-stream"}
   456→    |> put_session_header(state.session_id)
   457→    |> put_last_event_id_header(state.last_event_id)
   458→  end
   459→
   460→  defp process_sse_request(request, parent) do
   461→    case HTTP.follow_redirect(request) do
   462→      {:ok, %{status: 200, headers: resp_headers, body: body}} ->
   463→        handle_sse_response(resp_headers, body, parent)
   464→
   465→      {:ok, %{status: 405}} ->
   466→        Logging.transport_event(
   467→          "sse_not_supported",
   468→          "Server returned 405 for GET request"
   469→        )
   470→
   471→      error ->
   472→        Logging.transport_event("sse_connection_failed", %{error: error}, level: :warning)
   473→    end
   474→  end
   475→
   476→  defp handle_sse_response(headers, body, parent) do
   477→    if get_content_type(headers) == "text/event-stream" do
   478→      body
   479→      |> SSE.Parser.run()
   480→      |> Enum.each(fn event -> send(parent, {:sse_event, event}) end)
   481→    end
   482→  end
   483→
   484→  defp delete_session(state) do
   485→    headers = put_session_header(%{}, state.session_id)
   486→
   487→    options = [transport_opts: state.transport_opts] ++ state.http_options
   488→
   489→    request =
   490→      HTTP.build(:delete, URI.to_string(state.mcp_url), headers, nil, options)
   491→
   492→    case HTTP.follow_redirect(request) do
   493→      {:ok, %{status: status}} when status in [200, 405] ->
   494→        :ok
   495→
   496→      error ->
   497→        Logging.transport_event("session_delete_failed", %{error: error}, level: :debug)
   498→
   499→        :ok
   500→    end
   501→  end
   502→
   503→  defp put_last_event_id_header(headers, nil), do: headers
   504→
   505→  defp put_last_event_id_header(headers, event_id), do: Map.put(headers, "last-event-id", event_id)
   506→end
   507→

1→if Code.ensure_loaded?(Plug) do
     2→  defmodule Hermes.Server.Transport.StreamableHTTP.Plug do
     3→    @moduledoc """
     4→    A Plug implementation for the Streamable HTTP transport.
     5→
     6→    This plug handles the MCP Streamable HTTP protocol as specified in MCP 2025-03-26.
     7→    It provides a single endpoint that supports both GET and POST methods:
     8→
     9→    - GET: Opens an SSE stream for server-to-client communication
    10→    - POST: Handles JSON-RPC messages from client to server
    11→    - DELETE: Closes a session
    12→
    13→    ## SSE Streaming Architecture
    14→
    15→    This Plug handles SSE streaming by keeping the request process alive 
    16→    and managing the streaming loop for server-to-client communication.
    17→
    18→    ## Usage in Phoenix Router
    19→
    20→        pipeline :mcp do
    21→          plug :accepts, ["json"]
    22→        end
    23→
    24→        scope "/mcp" do
    25→          pipe_through :mcp
    26→          forward "/", to: Hermes.Server.Transport.StreamableHTTP.Plug, server: :your_server_name
    27→        end
    28→
    29→    ## Usage in Plug Router
    30→
    31→        forward "/mcp", to: Hermes.Server.Transport.StreamableHTTP.Plug, init_opts: [server: :your_server_name]
    32→
    33→    ## Configuration Options
    34→
    35→    - `:server` - The server process name (required)
    36→    - `:session_header` - Custom header name for session ID (default: "mcp-session-id")
    37→    - `:timeout` - Request timeout in milliseconds (default: 30000)
    38→    - `:registry` - The registry to use. See `Hermes.Server.Registry.Adapter` for more information (default: Elixir's Registry implementation)
    39→
    40→    ## Security Features
    41→
    42→    - Origin header validation for DNS rebinding protection
    43→    - Session-based request validation
    44→    - Automatic session cleanup on connection loss
    45→    - Rate limiting support (when configured)
    46→
    47→    ## HTTP Response Codes
    48→
    49→    - 200: Successful request
    50→    - 202: Accepted (for notifications and responses)
    51→    - 400: Bad request (malformed JSON-RPC)
    52→    - 404: Session not found
    53→    - 405: Method not allowed
    54→    - 500: Internal server error
    55→    """
    56→
    57→    @behaviour Plug
    58→
    59→    use Hermes.Logging
    60→
    61→    import Plug.Conn
    62→
    63→    alias Hermes.MCP.Error
    64→    alias Hermes.MCP.ID
    65→    alias Hermes.MCP.Message
    66→    alias Hermes.Server.Transport.StreamableHTTP
    67→    alias Hermes.SSE.Streaming
    68→    alias Plug.Conn.Unfetched
    69→
    70→    require Message
    71→
    72→    @default_session_header "mcp-session-id"
    73→    @default_timeout 30_000
    74→
    75→    # Plug callbacks
    76→
    77→    @impl Plug
    78→    def init(opts) do
    79→      server = Keyword.fetch!(opts, :server)
    80→      registry = Keyword.get(opts, :registry, Hermes.Server.Registry)
    81→      transport = registry.transport(server, :streamable_http)
    82→      session_header = Keyword.get(opts, :session_header, @default_session_header)
    83→      timeout = Keyword.get(opts, :timeout, @default_timeout)
    84→
    85→      %{transport: transport, session_header: session_header, timeout: timeout}
    86→    end
    87→
    88→    @impl Plug
    89→    def call(conn, opts) do
    90→      case conn.method do
    91→        "GET" -> handle_get(conn, opts)
    92→        "POST" -> handle_post(conn, opts)
    93→        "DELETE" -> handle_delete(conn, opts)
    94→        _ -> send_error(conn, 405, "Method not allowed")
    95→      end
    96→    end
    97→
    98→    # GET request handler - establishes SSE connection
    99→
   100→    defp handle_get(conn, %{transport: transport, session_header: session_header}) do
   101→      if wants_sse?(conn) do
   102→        session_id = get_or_create_session_id(conn, session_header)
   103→
   104→        case StreamableHTTP.register_sse_handler(transport, session_id) do
   105→          :ok ->
   106→            start_sse_streaming(conn, transport, session_id, session_header)
   107→
   108→          {:error, reason} ->
   109→            Logging.transport_event("sse_registration_failed", %{reason: reason}, level: :error)
   110→
   111→            send_error(conn, 500, "Could not establish SSE connection")
   112→        end
   113→      else
   114→        send_error(conn, 406, "Accept header must include text/event-stream")
   115→      end
   116→    end
   117→
   118→    # POST request handler - processes MCP messages
   119→
   120→    defp handle_post(conn, %{transport: transport, session_header: session_header} = opts) do
   121→      with :ok <- validate_accept_header(conn),
   122→           {:ok, body, conn} <- maybe_read_request_body(conn, opts),
   123→           {:ok, [message]} <- maybe_parse_messages(body) do
   124→        session_id = determine_session_id(conn, session_header, message)
   125→        context = build_request_context(conn)
   126→
   127→        Logging.transport_event("parsed_messages", %{
   128→          message: message,
   129→          session_id: session_id
   130→        })
   131→
   132→        process_message(message, conn, transport, session_id, context, session_header)
   133→      else
   134→        {:error, :invalid_accept_header} ->
   135→          send_error(
   136→            conn,
   137→            406,
   138→            "Not Acceptable: Client must accept both application/json and text/event-stream"
   139→          )
   140→
   141→        {:error, :invalid_json} ->
   142→          send_jsonrpc_error(
   143→            conn,
   144→            Error.protocol(:parse_error, %{message: "Invalid JSON"}),
   145→            nil
   146→          )
   147→
   148→        {:error, reason} ->
   149→          Logging.transport_event("request_error", %{reason: reason}, level: :error)
   150→
   151→          send_jsonrpc_error(
   152→            conn,
   153→            Error.protocol(:parse_error, %{reason: reason}),
   154→            nil
   155→          )
   156→      end
   157→    end
   158→
   159→    defp process_message(message, conn, transport, session_id, context, session_header) when is_map(message) do
   160→      if Message.is_request(message) do
   161→        handle_request_with_possible_sse(
   162→          conn,
   163→          transport,
   164→          session_id,
   165→          message,
   166→          context,
   167→          session_header
   168→        )
   169→      else
   170→        # Notification
   171→        transport
   172→        |> StreamableHTTP.handle_message(session_id, message, context)
   173→        |> format_notification_response(conn)
   174→      end
   175→    end
   176→
   177→    defp format_notification_response({:ok, _}, conn) do
   178→      conn
   179→      |> put_resp_content_type("application/json")
   180→      |> send_resp(202, "{}")
   181→    end
   182→
   183→    defp format_notification_response({:error, %Error{} = error}, conn) do
   184→      send_jsonrpc_error(conn, error, nil)
   185→    end
   186→
   187→    defp format_notification_response({:error, reason}, conn) do
   188→      Logging.transport_event("notification_handling_failed", %{reason: reason}, level: :error)
   189→
   190→      send_jsonrpc_error(
   191→        conn,
   192→        Error.protocol(:internal_error, %{reason: reason}),
   193→        nil
   194→      )
   195→    end
   196→
   197→    # DELETE request handler - closes session
   198→
   199→    defp handle_delete(conn, %{transport: transport, session_header: session_header}) do
   200→      case get_req_header(conn, session_header) do
   201→        [session_id] when is_binary(session_id) and session_id != "" ->
   202→          StreamableHTTP.unregister_sse_handler(transport, session_id)
   203→
   204→          conn
   205→          |> put_resp_content_type("application/json")
   206→          |> send_resp(200, "{}")
   207→
   208→        _ ->
   209→          send_error(conn, 400, "Session ID required")
   210→      end
   211→    end
   212→
   213→    # Handle requests that might need SSE streaming
   214→
   215→    defp handle_request_with_possible_sse(conn, transport, session_id, body, context, session_header) do
   216→      if wants_sse?(conn) do
   217→        handle_sse_request(
   218→          conn,
   219→          transport,
   220→          session_id,
   221→          body,
   222→          context,
   223→          session_header
   224→        )
   225→      else
   226→        handle_json_request(
   227→          conn,
   228→          transport,
   229→          session_id,
   230→          body,
   231→          context,
   232→          session_header
   233→        )
   234→      end
   235→    end
   236→
   237→    defp handle_sse_request(conn, transport, session_id, body, context, session_header) do
   238→      case StreamableHTTP.handle_message_for_sse(
   239→             transport,
   240→             session_id,
   241→             body,
   242→             context
   243→           ) do
   244→        {:sse, response} ->
   245→          route_sse_response(
   246→            conn,
   247→            transport,
   248→            session_id,
   249→            response,
   250→            body,
   251→            context,
   252→            session_header
   253→          )
   254→
   255→        {:ok, response} ->
   256→          conn
   257→          |> put_resp_content_type("application/json")
   258→          |> maybe_add_session_header(session_header, session_id)
   259→          |> send_resp(200, response)
   260→
   261→        {:error, error} ->
   262→          handle_request_error(conn, error, body)
   263→      end
   264→    end
   265→
   266→    defp handle_json_request(conn, transport, session_id, body, context, session_header) do
   267→      case StreamableHTTP.handle_message(transport, session_id, body, context) do
   268→        {:ok, response} ->
   269→          conn
   270→          |> put_resp_content_type("application/json")
   271→          |> maybe_add_session_header(session_header, session_id)
   272→          |> send_resp(200, response)
   273→
   274→        {:error, error} ->
   275→          handle_request_error(conn, error, body)
   276→      end
   277→    end
   278→
   279→    defp route_sse_response(conn, transport, session_id, response, body, context, session_header) do
   280→      if handler_pid = StreamableHTTP.get_sse_handler(transport, session_id) do
   281→        send(handler_pid, {:sse_message, response})
   282→
   283→        conn
   284→        |> put_resp_content_type("application/json")
   285→        |> send_resp(202, "{}")
   286→      else
   287→        establish_sse_for_request(
   288→          conn,
   289→          transport,
   290→          session_id,
   291→          body,
   292→          context,
   293→          session_header
   294→        )
   295→      end
   296→    end
   297→
   298→    defp handle_request_error(conn, %Error{} = error, body) do
   299→      send_jsonrpc_error(conn, error, extract_request_id(body))
   300→    end
   301→
   302→    defp handle_request_error(conn, reason, body) do
   303→      Logging.transport_event("request_error", %{reason: reason}, level: :error)
   304→
   305→      send_jsonrpc_error(
   306→        conn,
   307→        Error.protocol(:internal_error, %{reason: reason}),
   308→        extract_request_id(body)
   309→      )
   310→    end
   311→
   312→    defp establish_sse_for_request(conn, transport, session_id, body, context, session_header) do
   313→      case StreamableHTTP.register_sse_handler(transport, session_id) do
   314→        :ok ->
   315→          start_background_request(transport, session_id, body, context)
   316→          start_sse_streaming(conn, transport, session_id, session_header)
   317→
   318→        {:error, reason} ->
   319→          Logging.transport_event("sse_registration_failed", %{reason: reason}, level: :error)
   320→
   321→          send_jsonrpc_error(
   322→            conn,
   323→            Error.protocol(:internal_error, %{reason: reason}),
   324→            extract_request_id(body)
   325→          )
   326→      end
   327→    end
   328→
   329→    defp start_background_request(transport, session_id, body, context) do
   330→      self_pid = self()
   331→
   332→      Task.start(fn ->
   333→        case StreamableHTTP.handle_message(transport, session_id, body, context) do
   334→          {:ok, response} when is_binary(response) ->
   335→            send(self_pid, {:sse_message, response})
   336→
   337→          {:error, reason} ->
   338→            Logging.transport_event(
   339→              "sse_background_request_error",
   340→              %{reason: reason},
   341→              level: :error
   342→            )
   343→        end
   344→      end)
   345→    end
   346→
   347→    defp start_sse_streaming(conn, transport, session_id, session_header) do
   348→      conn
   349→      |> put_resp_header(session_header, session_id)
   350→      |> Streaming.prepare_connection()
   351→      |> Streaming.start(transport, session_id,
   352→        on_close: fn ->
   353→          StreamableHTTP.unregister_sse_handler(transport, session_id)
   354→        end
   355→      )
   356→    end
   357→
   358→    # Helper functions
   359→
   360→    defp wants_sse?(conn) do
   361→      conn
   362→      |> get_req_header("accept")
   363→      |> List.first("")
   364→      |> String.contains?("text/event-stream")
   365→    end
   366→
   367→    defp validate_accept_header(conn) do
   368→      accept_header =
   369→        conn
   370→        |> get_req_header("accept")
   371→        |> List.first("")
   372→
   373→      if String.contains?(accept_header, "application/json") and
   374→           String.contains?(accept_header, "text/event-stream") do
   375→        :ok
   376→      else
   377→        {:error, :invalid_accept_header}
   378→      end
   379→    end
   380→
   381→    defp get_or_create_session_id(conn, session_header) do
   382→      case get_req_header(conn, session_header) do
   383→        [session_id] when is_binary(session_id) and session_id != "" ->
   384→          session_id
   385→
   386→        _ ->
   387→          ID.generate_session_id()
   388→      end
   389→    end
   390→
   391→    defp determine_session_id(_conn, _header, [message]) when Message.is_initialize(message) do
   392→      ID.generate_session_id()
   393→    end
   394→
   395→    defp determine_session_id(conn, session_header, _message) do
   396→      get_or_create_session_id(conn, session_header)
   397→    end
   398→
   399→    defp maybe_parse_messages(body) when is_binary(body) do
   400→      case Message.decode(body) do
   401→        {:ok, messages} ->
   402→          {:ok, messages}
   403→
   404→        {:error, reason} ->
   405→          Logging.transport_event(
   406→            "parse_error",
   407→            %{body: body, reason: inspect(reason)},
   408→            level: :error
   409→          )
   410→
   411→          {:error, :invalid_json}
   412→      end
   413→    end
   414→
   415→    defp maybe_parse_messages(body) when is_map(body) do
   416→      case Message.validate_message(body) do
   417→        {:ok, message} -> {:ok, [message]}
   418→        {:error, _} -> {:error, :invalid_json}
   419→      end
   420→    end
   421→
   422→    defp maybe_add_session_header(conn, session_header, session_id) do
   423→      if get_req_header(conn, session_header) == [] do
   424→        put_resp_header(conn, session_header, session_id)
   425→      else
   426→        conn
   427→      end
   428→    end
   429→
   430→    defp maybe_read_request_body(%{body_params: %Unfetched{aspect: :body_params}} = conn, %{timeout: timeout}) do
   431→      case Plug.Conn.read_body(conn, read_timeout: timeout) do
   432→        {:ok, body, conn} -> {:ok, body, conn}
   433→        {:error, reason} -> {:error, reason}
   434→      end
   435→    end
   436→
   437→    defp maybe_read_request_body(%{body_params: body} = conn, _), do: {:ok, body, conn}
   438→
   439→    defp send_error(conn, status, message) do
   440→      data = %{data: %{message: message, http_status: status}}
   441→
   442→      mcp_error =
   443→        case status do
   444→          405 -> Error.protocol(:method_not_found, data)
   445→          406 -> Error.protocol(:invalid_request, data)
   446→          _ -> Error.protocol(:internal_error, data)
   447→        end
   448→
   449→      {:ok, error_response} = Error.to_json_rpc(mcp_error, ID.generate_error_id())
   450→
   451→      conn
   452→      |> put_resp_content_type("application/json")
   453→      |> send_resp(status, error_response)
   454→    end
   455→
   456→    defp send_jsonrpc_error(conn, %Error{} = error, id) do
   457→      error_id = id || ID.generate_error_id()
   458→      {:ok, encoded_error} = Error.to_json_rpc(error, error_id)
   459→
   460→      conn
   461→      |> put_resp_content_type("application/json")
   462→      |> send_resp(400, encoded_error)
   463→    end
   464→
   465→    defp extract_request_id(%{"id" => request_id}), do: request_id
   466→    defp extract_request_id(_), do: nil
   467→
   468→    defp build_request_context(conn) do
   469→      %{
   470→        assigns: conn.assigns,
   471→        type: :http,
   472→        req_headers: conn.req_headers,
   473→        query_params: fetch_query_params_safe(conn),
   474→        remote_ip: conn.remote_ip,
   475→        scheme: conn.scheme,
   476→        host: conn.host,
   477→        port: conn.port,
   478→        request_path: conn.request_path
   479→      }
   480→    end
   481→
   482→    defp fetch_query_params_safe(conn) do
   483→      case conn.query_params do
   484→        %Unfetched{} -> nil
   485→        params -> params
   486→      end
   487→    end
   488→  end
   489→end
   490→

1→defmodule Hermes.Server.Transport.StreamableHTTP do
     2→  @moduledoc """
     3→  StreamableHTTP transport implementation for MCP servers.
     4→
     5→  This module provides an HTTP-based transport layer that supports multiple
     6→  concurrent client sessions through Server-Sent Events (SSE). It enables
     7→  web-based MCP clients to communicate with the server using standard HTTP
     8→  protocols.
     9→
    10→  ## Features
    11→
    12→  - Multiple concurrent client sessions
    13→  - Server-Sent Events for real-time server-to-client communication
    14→  - HTTP POST endpoint for client-to-server messages
    15→  - Automatic session cleanup on disconnect
    16→  - Integration with Phoenix/Plug applications
    17→
    18→  ## Usage
    19→
    20→  StreamableHTTP is typically started through the server supervisor:
    21→
    22→      Hermes.Server.start_link(MyServer, [],
    23→        transport: :streamable_http,
    24→        streamable_http: [port: 4000]
    25→      )
    26→
    27→  For integration with existing Phoenix/Plug applications:
    28→
    29→      # In your router
    30→      forward "/mcp", Hermes.Server.Transport.StreamableHTTP.Plug,
    31→        server: MyApp.MCPServer
    32→
    33→  ## Message Flow
    34→
    35→  1. Client connects to `/sse` endpoint, receives a session ID
    36→  2. Client sends messages via POST to `/messages` with session ID header
    37→  3. Server responses are pushed through the SSE connection
    38→  4. Connection closes on client disconnect or server shutdown
    39→
    40→  ## Configuration
    41→
    42→  - `:port` - HTTP server port (default: 4000)
    43→  - `:server` - The MCP server process to connect to
    44→  - `:name` - Process registration name
    45→  """
    46→
    47→  @behaviour Hermes.Transport.Behaviour
    48→
    49→  use GenServer
    50→  use Hermes.Logging
    51→
    52→  import Peri
    53→
    54→  alias Hermes.MCP.Message
    55→  alias Hermes.Telemetry
    56→  alias Hermes.Transport.Behaviour, as: Transport
    57→
    58→  require Message
    59→
    60→  @type t :: GenServer.server()
    61→
    62→  @typedoc """
    63→  StreamableHTTP transport options
    64→
    65→  - `:server` - The server process (required)
    66→  - `:name` - Name for registering the GenServer (required)
    67→  """
    68→  @type option ::
    69→          {:server, GenServer.server()}
    70→          | {:name, GenServer.name()}
    71→          | GenServer.option()
    72→
    73→  defschema(:parse_options, [
    74→    {:server, {:required, Hermes.get_schema(:process_name)}},
    75→    {:name, {:required, {:custom, &Hermes.genserver_name/1}}},
    76→    {:registry, {:atom, {:default, Hermes.Server.Registry}}},
    77→    {:request_timeout, {:integer, {:default, to_timeout(second: 30)}}},
    78→    {:task_supervisor, {:required, {:custom, &Hermes.genserver_name/1}}}
    79→  ])
    80→
    81→  @doc """
    82→  Starts the StreamableHTTP transport.
    83→  """
    84→  @impl Transport
    85→  @spec start_link(Enumerable.t(option())) :: GenServer.on_start()
    86→  def start_link(opts) do
    87→    opts = parse_options!(opts)
    88→    {name, opts} = Keyword.pop!(opts, :name)
    89→
    90→    GenServer.start_link(__MODULE__, Map.new(opts), name: name)
    91→  end
    92→
    93→  @doc """
    94→  Sends a message to the client via the active SSE connection.
    95→
    96→  This function is used for server-initiated notifications.
    97→  It will broadcast to all active SSE connections.
    98→
    99→  ## Parameters

   202→  @impl GenServer

   220→      server: server

   227→    )

   232→  @impl GenServer

   274→  end
   275→

    30→  @doc """
    31→  Starts a new session agent with initial state.

    33→  @spec start_link(keyword()) :: Agent.on_start()

    38→    Agent.start_link(fn -> new(id: session_id, name: name) end, name: name)

    60→  @doc """

    62→
    63→  This function:

    68→  @spec update_from_initialization(GenServer.name(), String.t(), map, map) :: :ok
    69→  def update_from_initialization(session, negotiated_version, client_info, capabilities) do
    70→    Agent.update(session, fn state ->
    71→      %{
    72→        state
    73→        | protocol_version: negotiated_version,
    74→          client_info: client_info,
    75→          client_capabilities: capabilities
    76→      }
    77→    end)
    78→  end
    79→
    80→  @doc """
    81→  Marks the session as initialized.
    82→  """
    83→  @spec mark_initialized(GenServer.name()) :: :ok
    84→  def mark_initialized(session) do

    86→  end
    87→
    88→  @doc """
    89→  Updates the log level.
    90→  """
    91→  @spec set_log_level(GenServer.name(), String.t()) :: :ok

    96→  @doc """
    97→  Tracks a new pending request in the session.
    98→  """
    99→  @spec track_request(GenServer.name(), String.t(), String.t()) :: :ok

   105→      }

   107→      %{
   108→        state
   109→        | pending_requests: Map.put(state.pending_requests, request_id, request_info)
   110→      }
   111→    end)
   112→  end
   113→
   114→  @doc """
   115→  Removes a completed request from tracking.
   116→  """
   117→  @spec complete_request(GenServer.name(), String.t()) :: map() | nil
   118→  def complete_request(session, request_id) do
   119→    Agent.get_and_update(session, fn state ->

   122→    end)
   123→  end
   124→
   125→  @doc """

   128→  @spec has_pending_request?(GenServer.name(), String.t()) :: boolean()

   133→  end
   134→
   135→  @doc """
   136→  Gets all pending requests for a session.

   138→  @spec get_pending_requests(GenServer.name()) :: map()
   139→  def get_pending_requests(session) do

   141→  end
   142→end

## Summary

Based on my thorough exploration of the hermes_mcp dependency (version 0.14.1), here is comprehensive documentation on StreamableHTTP transport setup, SSE vs JSON response handling, and session management:

### 1. StreamableHTTP Transport Setup and Configuration

**File:** `/Users/pepicrft/src/github.com/tuist/tuist3/server/deps/hermes_mcp/lib/hermes/transport/streamable_http.ex`

The StreamableHTTP transport accepts the following configuration options:

```elixir
@typedoc """
The options for the Streamable HTTP transport.

- `:base_url` - The base URL of the MCP server (e.g. http://localhost:8000) (required).
- `:mcp_path` - The MCP endpoint path (e.g. /mcp) (default "/").
- `:client` - The client to send the messages to.
- `:headers` - The headers to send with the HTTP requests.
- `:transport_opts` - The underlying HTTP transport options.
- `:http_options` - The underlying HTTP client options.
- `:enable_sse` - Whether to establish a GET connection for server-initiated messages (default false).
"""
```

Key configuration: `enable_sse` controls whether to establish a separate GET connection for server-initiated messages (line 69).

### 2. SSE Streaming vs JSON Response Handling

**The HTTP Accept Header Determines Response Type:**

From the Plug implementation (`/Users/pepicrft/src/github.com/tuist/tuist3/server/deps/hermes_mcp/lib/hermes/server/transport/streamable_http/plug.ex`, lines 360-365):

```elixir
defp wants_sse?(conn) do
  conn
  |> get_req_header("accept")
  |> List.first("")
  |> String.contains?("text/event-stream")
end
```

**Response Handling Logic (lines 215-235):**

1. **If Accept header contains "text/event-stream"**: The server attempts SSE streaming via `handle_sse_request/6`
2. **If Accept header does NOT contain "text/event-stream"**: The server uses `handle_json_request/6` for simple JSON responses

**HTTP Response Codes:**
- **200 OK with application/json**: Single JSON response (document string 62 in streamable_http.ex)
- **200 OK with text/event-stream**: SSE stream parsed and events forwarded to client
- **202 Accepted**: Message acknowledged, no immediate response (when handling notifications)
- **404 Not Found**: Session expired

### 3. Session Management

**File:** `/Users/pepicrft/src/github.com/tuist/tuist3/server/deps/hermes_mcp/lib/hermes/server/session.ex`

Sessions are managed through the `mcp-session-id` HTTP header. From the client transport (lines 369-377):

```elixir
defp put_session_header(headers, nil), do: headers
defp put_session_header(headers, session_id), do: Map.put(headers, "mcp-session-id", session_id)

defp update_session_id(state, headers) do
  case get_header(headers, "mcp-session-id") do
    nil -> state
    session_id -> %{state | session_id: session_id}
  end
end
```

**Session Features:**
- Session ID automatically extracted from server response headers
- Session ID included in subsequent requests
- Maintains session state throughout connection lifecycle
- Automatic session cleanup on disconnect (DELETE request)
- Server session expiration on idle (configurable) - noted in CHANGELOG as feature #143

### 4. No "Stateless" Mode, but JSON-Only vs SSE Options

The library does not have a "stateless" or "json mode" flag. Instead, the response type is determined entirely by the **Accept header**:

- **Accept: application/json** alone → returns plain JSON response (line 226 in plug.ex)
- **Accept: application/json, text/event-stream** → can return either SSE (if handler exists) or JSON

From Plug validation (lines 367-378):

```elixir
defp validate_accept_header(conn) do
  accept_header =
    conn
    |> get_req_header("accept")
    |> List.first("")

  if String.contains?(accept_header, "application/json") and
       String.contains?(accept_header, "text/event-stream") do
    :ok
  else
    {:error, :invalid_accept_header}
  end
end
```

**Important:** POST requests MUST include BOTH `application/json` AND `text/event-stream` in the Accept header (CHANGELOG entry #134).

### 5. SSE Handler Registration and Response Routing

**File:** `/Users/pepicrft/src/github.com/tuist/tuist3/server/deps/hermes_mcp/lib/hermes/server/transport/streamable_http.ex`

The transport distinguishes between:
- `handle_message/4` - for normal JSON response requests
- `handle_message_for_sse/4` - for requests that might stream via SSE (lines 169-175)

If an SSE handler is registered for a session, the response is routed via SSE (202 Accepted); otherwise, a regular JSON response is returned.

### 6. README and Documentation

From `/Users/pepicrft/src/github.com/tuist/tuist3/server/deps/hermes_mcp/README.md`:

- **Server setup:** `forward "/mcp", to: Hermes.Server.Transport.StreamableHTTP.Plug, init_opts: [server: MyApp.MCPServer]`
- **Client setup:** `{MyApp.MCPClient, transport: {:streamable_http, base_url: "http://localhost:4000"}}`
- Three working examples referenced: upcase-server (plug + streamable_http), echo-elixir (phoenix + sse), ascii-server (phoenix_live_view + streamable_http + UI)

### 7. CHANGELOG Highlights on Streaming

- **#187:** SSE transport deprecated in favor of StreamableHTTP
- **#134:** "client should send both sse/json headers on POST requests"
- **#143:** "server session expiration on idle (configurable)"
- **#25:** "allow sse graceful shutdown and handle sse streaming reconnection"
- **#203:** "streamable http on hermes cli (standalone)"

The key finding: the **client-side** `StreamableHTTP` transport has an `enable_sse` option (default `false`). When `false`, the client doesn't include `text/event-stream` in the `Accept` header, so responses come back as plain JSON.

But the **server-side** Plug at line 367-378 of `plug.ex` **requires** both `application/json` AND `text/event-stream` in the `Accept` header for POST requests, otherwise it returns a 406 error. This means any conforming client must send both, which triggers the SSE path.

Based on my search results, here's what I found about your query:

## Hermes MCP Latest Version

The latest version of hermes_mcp is v0.14.1. You can add it to your Elixir dependencies with {:hermes_mcp, "~> 0.14.1"}.

Hermes MCP is a Model Context Protocol (MCP) implementation in Elixir with Phoenix integration.

## Streamable HTTP vs SSE and Related Issues

The transition from Server-Sent Events (SSE) to Streamable HTTP as the preferred transport mechanism was introduced in the MCP specification update on March 26, 2025 (version 2025-03-26).

Streamable HTTP replaces the HTTP+SSE transport from protocol version 2024-11-05.

### Key Changes with Streamable HTTP:

Unlike traditional HTTP or SSE, Streamable HTTP enables true bidirectional communication, with servers able to send notifications or request additional information from clients on the same connection.

The older SSE approach had three major limitations: no support for resumable streams, requires the server to maintain a long-lived, highly available connection, and only allows server messages to be delivered via SSE.

### SSE Stream Issues (Hang):

I found one reported issue in the search results where users experienced "StreamableHTTPError: Streamable HTTP error: Failed to open SSE stream: Not Found", though this appears to be related to improper endpoint configuration rather than a general "hang" problem.

results:
Based on my search results, here's what I found about your query:

## Hermes MCP Latest Version

The latest version of hermes_mcp is v0.14.1. You can add it to your Elixir dependencies with {:hermes_mcp, "~> 0.14.1"}.

Hermes MCP is a Model Context Protocol (MCP) implementation in Elixir with Phoenix integration.

## Streamable HTTP vs SSE and Related Issues

The transition from Server-Sent Events (SSE) to Streamable HTTP as the preferred transport mechanism was introduced in the MCP specification update on March 26, 2025 (version 2025-03-26).

Streamable HTTP replaces the HTTP+SSE transport from protocol version 2024-11-05.

### Key Changes with Streamable HTTP:

Unlike traditional HTTP or SSE, Streamable HTTP enables true bidirectional communication, with servers able to send notifications or request additional information from clients on the same connection.

The older SSE approach had three major limitations: no support for resumable streams, requires the server to maintain a long-lived, highly available connection, and only allows server messages to be delivered via SSE.

### SSE Stream Issues (Hang):

I found one reported issue in the search results where users experienced "StreamableHTTPError: Streamable HTTP error: Failed to open SSE stream: Not Found", though this appears to be related to improper endpoint configuration rather than a general "hang" problem.

Hermes MCP supports streamable_http transport with a client configuration example, suggesting the library has built-in support for the modern Streamable HTTP protocol.

content.content:
> ## Documentation Index > Fetch the complete documentation index at: https://modelcontextprotocol.io/llms.txt > Use this file to discover all available pages before exploring further. # Transports **Protocol Revision**: 2025-03-26 MCP uses JSON-RPC to encode messages. JSON-RPC messages **MUST** be UTF-8 encoded. The protocol currently defines two standard transport mechanisms for client-server communication: 1. [stdio](#stdio), communication over standard in and standard out 2. [Streamable HTTP](#streamable-http) Clients **SHOULD** support stdio whenever possible. It is also possible for clients and servers to implement [custom transports](#custom-transports) in a pluggable fashion. ## stdio In the **stdio** transport: * The client launches the MCP server as a subprocess. * The server reads JSON-RPC messages from its standard input (`stdin`) and sends messages to its standard output (`stdout`). * Messages may be JSON-RPC requests, notifications, responses—or a JSON-RPC [batch](https://www.jsonrpc.org/specification#batch) containing one or more requests and/or notifications. * Messages are delimited by newlines, and **MUST NOT** contain embedded newlines. * The server **MAY** write UTF-8 strings to its standard error (`stderr`) for logging purposes. Clients **MAY** capture, forward, or ignore this logging. * The server **MUST NOT** write anything to its `stdout` that is not a valid MCP message. * The client **MUST NOT** write anything to the server's `stdin` that is not a valid MCP message. ```mermaid theme={null} sequenceDiagram participant Client participant Server Process Client->>+Server Process: Launch subprocess loop Message Exchange Client->>Server Process: Write to stdin Server Process->>Client: Write to stdout Server Process--)Client: Optional logs on stderr end Client->>Server Process: Close stdin, terminate subprocess deactivate Server Process ``` ## Streamable HTTP This replaces the [HTTP+SSE transport](/specification/2024-11-05/basic/transports#http-with-sse) from protocol version 2024-11-05. See the [backwards compatibility](#backwards-compatibility) guide below. In the **Streamable HTTP** transport, the server operates as an independent process that can handle multiple client connections. This transport uses HTTP POST and GET requests. Server can optionally make use of [Server-Sent Events](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Server-sent_events) (SSE) to stream multiple server messages. This permits basic MCP servers, as well as more feature-rich servers supporting streaming and server-to-client notifications and requests. The server **MUST** provide a single HTTP endpoint path (hereafter referred to as the **MCP endpoint**) that supports both POST and GET methods. For example, this could be a URL like `https://example.com/mcp`. #### Security Warning When implementing Streamable HTTP transport: 1. Servers **MUST** validate the `Origin` header on all incoming connections to prevent DNS rebinding attacks 2. When running locally, servers **SHOULD** bind only to localhost (127.0.0.1) rather than all network interfaces (0.0.0.0) 3. Servers **SHOULD** implement proper authentication for all connections Without these protections, attackers could use DNS rebinding to interact with local MCP servers from remote websites. ### Sending Messages to the Server Every JSON-RPC message sent from the client **MUST** be a new HTTP POST request to the MCP endpoint. 1. The client **MUST** use HTTP POST to send JSON-RPC messages to the MCP endpoint. 2. The client **MUST** include an `Accept` header, listing both `application/json` and `text/event-stream` as supported content types. 3. The body of the POST request **MUST** be one of the following: * A single JSON-RPC *request*, *notification*, or *response* * An array [batching](https://www.jsonrpc.org/specification#batch) one or more *requests and/or notifications* * An array [batching](https://www.jsonrpc.org/specification#batch) one or more *responses* 4. If the input consists solely of (any number of) JSON-RPC *responses* or *notifications*: * If the server accepts the input, the server **MUST** return HTTP status code 202 Accepted with no body. * If the server cannot accept the input, it **MUST** return an HTTP error status code (e.g., 400 Bad Request). The HTTP response body **MAY** comprise a JSON-RPC *error response* that has no `id`. 5. If the input contains any number of JSON-RPC *requests*, the server **MUST** either return `Content-Type: text/event-stream`, to initiate an SSE stream, or `Content-Type: application/json`, to return one JSON object. The client **MUST** support both these cases. 6. If the server initiates an SSE stream: * The SSE stream **SHOULD** eventually include one JSON-RPC *response* per each JSON-RPC *request* sent in the POST body. These *responses* **MAY** be [batched](https://www.jsonrpc.org/specification#batch). * The server **MAY** send JSON-RPC *requests* and *notifications* before sending a JSON-RPC *response*. These messages **SHOULD** relate to the originating client *request*. These *requests* and *notifications* **MAY** be [batched](https://www.jsonrpc.org/specification#batch). * The server **SHOULD NOT** close the SSE stream before sending a JSON-RPC *response* per each received JSON-RPC *request*, unless the [session](#session-management) expires. * After all JSON-RPC *responses* have been sent, the server **SHOULD** close the SSE stream. * Disconnection **MAY** occur at any time (e.g., due to network conditions). Therefore: * Disconnection **SHOULD NOT** be interpreted as the client cancelling its request. * To cancel, the client **SHOULD** explicitly send an MCP `CancelledNotification`. * To avoid message loss due to disconnection, the server **MAY** make the stream [resumable](#resumability-and-redelivery). ### Listening for Messages from the Server 1. The client **MAY** issue an HTTP GET to the MCP endpoint. This can be used to open an SSE stream, allowing the server to communicate to the client, without the client first sending data via HTTP POST. 2. The client **MUST** include an `Accept` header, listing `text/event-stream` as a supported content type. 3. The server **MUST** either return `Content-Type: text/event-stream` in response to this HTTP GET, or else return HTTP 405 Method Not Allowed, indicating that the server does not offer an SSE stream at this endpoint. 4. If the server initiates an SSE stream: * The server **MAY** send JSON-RPC *requests* and *notifications* on the stream. These *requests* and *notifications* **MAY** be [batched](https://www.jsonrpc.org/specification#batch). * These messages **SHOULD** be unrelated to any concurrently-running JSON-RPC *request* from the client. * The server **MUST NOT** send a JSON-RPC *response* on the stream **unless** [resuming](#resumability-and-redelivery) a stream associated with a previous client request. * The server **MAY** close the SSE stream at any time. * The client **MAY** close the SSE stream at any time. ### Multiple Connections 1. The client **MAY** remain connected to multiple SSE streams simultaneously. 2. The server **MUST** send each of its JSON-RPC messages on only one of the connected streams; that is, it **MUST NOT** broadcast the same message across multiple streams. * The risk of message loss **MAY** be mitigated by making the stream [resumable](#resumability-and-redelivery). ### Resumability and Redelivery To support resuming broken connections, and redelivering messages that might otherwise be lost: 1. Servers **MAY** attach an `id` field to their SSE events, as described in the [SSE standard](https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/server-sent-events.html#event-stream-interpretation). * If present, the ID **MUST** be globally unique across all streams within that [session](#session-management)—or all streams with that specific client, if session management is not in use. 2. If the client wishes to resume after a broken connection, it **SHOULD** issue an HTTP GET to the MCP endpoint, and include the [`Last-Event-ID`](https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/server-sent-events.html#the-last-event-id-header) header to indicate the last event ID it received. * The server **MAY** use this header to replay messages that would have been sent after the last event ID, *on the stream that was disconnected*, and to resume the stream from that point. * The server **MUST NOT** replay messages that would have been delivered on a different stream. In other words, these event IDs should be assigned by servers on a *per-stream* basis, to act as a cursor within that particular stream. ### Session Management An MCP "session" consists of logically related interactions between a client and a server, beginning with the [initialization phase](/specification/2025-03-26/basic/lifecycle). To support servers which want to establish stateful sessions: 1. A server using the Streamable HTTP transport **MAY** assign a session ID at initialization time, by including it in an `Mcp-Session-Id` header on the HTTP response containing the `InitializeResult`. * The session ID **SHOULD** be globally unique and cryptographically secure (e.g., a securely generated UUID, a JWT, or a cryptographic hash). * The session ID **MUST** only contain visible ASCII characters (ranging from 0x21 to 0x7E). 2. If an `Mcp-Session-Id` is returned by the server during initialization, clients using the Streamable HTTP transport **MUST** include it in the `Mcp-Session-Id` header on all of their subsequent HTTP requests. * Servers that require a session ID **SHOULD** respond to requests without an `Mcp-Session-Id` header (other than initialization) with HTTP 400 Bad Request. 3. The server **MAY** terminate the session at any time, after which it **MUST** respond to requests containing that session ID with HTTP 404 Not Found. 4. When a client receives HTTP 404 in response to a request containing an `Mcp-Session-Id`, it **MUST** start a new session by sending a new `InitializeRequest` without a session ID attached. 5. Clients that no longer need a particular session (e.g., because the user is leaving the client application) **SHOULD** send an HTTP DELETE to the MCP endpoint with the `Mcp-Session-Id` header, to explicitly terminate the session. * The server **MAY** respond to this request with HTTP 405 Method Not Allowed, indicating that the server does not allow clients to terminate sessions. ### Sequence Diagram ```mermaid theme={null} sequenceDiagram participant Client participant Server note over Client, Server: initialization Client->>+Server: POST InitializeRequest Server->>-Client: InitializeResponse
Mcp-Session-Id: 1868a90c... Client->>+Server: POST InitializedNotification
Mcp-Session-Id: 1868a90c... Server->>-Client: 202 Accepted note over Client, Server: client requests Client->>+Server: POST ... request ...
Mcp-Session-Id: 1868a90c... alt single HTTP response Server->>Client: ... response ... else server opens SSE stream loop while connection remains open Server-)Client: ... SSE messages from server ... end Server-)Client: SSE event: ... response ... end deactivate Server note over Client, Server: client notifications/responses Client->>+Server: POST ... notification/response ...
Mcp-Session-Id: 1868a90c... Server->>-Client: 202 Accepted note over Client, Server: server requests Client->>+Server: GET
Mcp-Session-Id: 1868a90c... loop while connection remains open Server-)Client: ... SSE messages from server ... end deactivate Server ``` ### Backwards Compatibility Clients and servers can maintain backwards compatibility with the deprecated [HTTP+SSE transport](/specification/2024-11-05/basic/transports#http-with-sse) (from protocol version 2024-11-05) as follows: **Servers** wanting to support older clients should: * Continue to host both the SSE and POST endpoints of the old transport, alongside the new "MCP endpoint" defined for the Streamable HTTP transport. * It is also possible to combine the old POST endpoint and the new MCP endpoint, but this may introduce unneeded complexity. **Clients** wanting to support older servers should: 1. Accept an MCP server URL from the user, which may point to either a server using the old transport or the new transport. 2. Attempt to POST an `InitializeRequest` to the server URL, with an `Accept` header as defined above: * If it succeeds, the client can assume this is a server supporting the new Streamable HTTP transport. * If it fails with an HTTP 4xx status code (e.g., 405 Method Not Allowed or 404 Not Found): * Issue a GET request to the server URL, expecting that this will open an SSE stream and return an `endpoint` event as the first event. * When the `endpoint` event arrives, the client can assume this is a server running the old HTTP+SSE transport, and should use that transport for all subsequent communication. ## Custom Transports Clients and servers **MAY** implement additional custom transport mechanisms to suit their specific needs. The protocol is transport-agnostic and can be implemented over any communication channel that supports bidirectional message exchange. Implementers who choose to support custom transports **MUST** ensure they preserve the JSON-RPC message format and lifecycle requirements defined by MCP. Custom transports **SHOULD** document their specific connection establishment and message exchange patterns to aid interoperability.

Attempt to POST an `InitializeRequest` to the server URL, with an `Accept` header as defined above: * If it succeeds, the client can assume this is a server supporting the new Streamable HTTP transport. * If it fails with an HTTP 4xx status code (e.g., 405 Method Not Allowed or 404 Not Found): * Issue a GET request to the server URL, expecting that this will open an SSE stream and return an `endpoint` event as the first event. * When the `endpoint` event arrives, the client can assume this is a server running the old HTTP+SSE transport, and should use that transport for all subsequent communication. ## Custom Transports Clients and servers **MAY** implement additional custom transport mechanisms to suit their specific needs. The protocol is transport-agnostic and can be implemented over any communication channel that supports bidirectional message exchange. Implementers who choose to support custom transports **MUST** ensure they preserve the JSON-RPC message format and lifecycle requirements defined by MCP. Custom transports **SHOULD** document their specific connection establishment and message exchange patterns to aid interoperability.

content.text:
The MCP spec is very clear on this. Here's the key passage:

> **Point 5:** If the input contains any number of JSON-RPC *requests*, the server **MUST** either return `Content-Type: text/event-stream`, to initiate an SSE stream, or `Content-Type: application/json`, to return one JSON object. The client **MUST** support both these cases.

And critically:

> **Point 6:** After all JSON-RPC *responses* have been sent, the server **SHOULD** close the SSE stream.

So the MCP spec says:
1. The server **can choose** to respond with plain JSON (`application/json`) instead of SSE for any request
2. If it does use SSE, it **should close the stream** after all responses are sent

The bug is in hermes_mcp's `establish_sse_for_request` -- it opens an SSE stream for the response but **never closes it** after sending the response. The `Streaming.loop` keeps running indefinitely.

Looking at the code in `establish_sse_for_request` (plug.ex line 312-327), the background task sends `{:sse_message, response}` to the streaming loop, but nobody sends `:close_sse` afterward. The fix should be in the hermes_mcp library itself: after the background task delivers the response, it should also send `:close_sse` to terminate the stream.

You could either:
1. **Open an issue/PR on [cloudwalk/hermes-mcp](https://github.com/cloudwalk/hermes-mcp)** to fix `start_background_request` to send `:close_sse` after the response
2. **Work around it** by overriding the Plug behavior in your server to use JSON responses instead of SSE for simple tool calls

Would you like me to implement a workaround, or would you prefer to report this upstream?

Looking at the plug code again, the issue is in `validate_accept_header` (line 367) which **requires** both `application/json` and `text/event-stream`. If I strip `text/event-stream`, the Hermes plug will return a 406 error.

So the right approach is to write a wrapper plug that intercepts POST requests and delegates to the Hermes plug but overrides the `Accept` header to only contain `application/json` for POST requests. But we need to also bypass the `validate_accept_header` check.

Let me re-read the relevant paths more carefully.

    defp handle_post(conn, %{transport: transport, session_header: session_header} = opts) do
      with :ok <- validate_accept_header(conn),
           {:ok, body, conn} <- maybe_read_request_body(conn, opts),
           {:ok, [message]} <- maybe_parse_messages(body) do
        session_id = determine_session_id(conn, session_header, message)

      else

content.text:
The `validate_accept_header` requires both, and `wants_sse?` checks for `text/event-stream`. The simplest fix: replace the `Accept` header with just `application/json, text/event-stream` on the way in (to pass validation), but then also set a flag that the wrapper plug can use... Actually, that won't work since `wants_sse?` reads the header directly.

The cleanest solution: write a custom plug that wraps the Hermes plug and, for POST requests, replaces the `Accept` header to keep `application/json` but remove `text/event-stream`. Then it also needs to pass the validation. Since the validation is private to the Hermes plug, the best approach is to write our own plug that replicates the needed behavior but forces JSON responses.

Actually, the simplest approach: modify the `Accept` header to pass validation but not trigger SSE. Looking at the code:

- `validate_accept_header` checks `String.contains?(accept_header, "application/json") and String.contains?(accept_header, "text/event-stream")`  
- `wants_sse?` checks `String.contains?(accept_header, "text/event-stream")`

These use the same check, so there's no way to pass validation without triggering SSE through header manipulation alone.

The real fix: a wrapper plug that sets the `Accept` header to include both (for validation) but then after parsing, replaces it to remove `text/event-stream`. But since the Hermes plug reads it in multiple places within the same `call`, a simpler approach is a custom plug.

input.content:
defmodule TuistWeb.Plugs.MCPJsonResponsePlug do
  @moduledoc """
  A plug that wraps the Hermes StreamableHTTP plug and forces JSON responses
  instead of SSE streams for POST requests.

  The MCP spec allows servers to respond to POST requests with either
  `application/json` or `text/event-stream`. The Hermes plug defaults to SSE
  when the client's Accept header includes `text/event-stream`, which opens a
  streaming loop that never closes for simple request/response tool calls.

  This plug rewrites the Accept header on POST requests to remove
  `text/event-stream`, forcing the Hermes plug down the plain JSON response
  path. The original Accept header is preserved so that the Hermes validation
  still sees the required content types in the request context.
  """

  @behaviour Plug

  alias Hermes.Server.Transport.StreamableHTTP.Plug, as: HermesPlug

  @impl Plug
  def init(opts), do: HermesPlug.init(opts)

  @impl Plug
  def call(%Plug.Conn{method: "POST"} = conn, opts) do
    conn
    |> force_json_accept()
    |> HermesPlug.call(opts)
  end

  def call(conn, opts), do: HermesPlug.call(conn, opts)

  defp force_json_accept(conn) do
    %{conn | req_headers: replace_accept_header(conn.req_headers)}
  end

  defp replace_accept_header(headers) do
    Enum.map(headers, fn
      {"accept", _value} -> {"accept", "application/json"}
      other -> other
    end)
  end
end

defmodule TuistWeb.Plugs.MCPJsonResponsePlug do
  @moduledoc """
  A plug that wraps the Hermes StreamableHTTP plug and forces JSON responses
  instead of SSE streams for POST requests.

  The MCP spec allows servers to respond to POST requests with either
  `application/json` or `text/event-stream`. The Hermes plug defaults to SSE
  when the client's Accept header includes `text/event-stream`, which opens a
  streaming loop that never closes for simple request/response tool calls.

  This plug handles POST requests directly using the StreamableHTTP public API,
  always returning plain JSON responses. GET and DELETE requests are delegated
  to the Hermes plug as-is.
  """

  @behaviour Plug

  import Plug.Conn

  alias Hermes.MCP.Error
  alias Hermes.MCP.ID
  alias Hermes.MCP.Message
  alias Hermes.Server.Transport.StreamableHTTP
  alias Hermes.Server.Transport.StreamableHTTP.Plug, as: HermesPlug
  alias Plug.Conn.Unfetched

  require Message

  @default_session_header "mcp-session-id"

  @impl Plug
  def init(opts), do: HermesPlug.init(opts)

  @impl Plug
  def call(%Plug.Conn{method: "POST"} = conn, opts) do
    handle_post(conn, opts)
  end

  def call(conn, opts), do: HermesPlug.call(conn, opts)

  defp handle_post(conn, %{transport: transport, session_header: session_header, timeout: timeout}) do
    with {:ok, body, conn} <- maybe_read_body(conn, timeout),
         {:ok, [message]} <- parse_message(body) do
      session_id = get_or_create_session_id(conn, session_header)
      context = build_request_context(conn)

      if Message.is_request(message) do
        handle_request(conn, transport, session_id, message, context, session_header)
      else
        handle_notification(conn, transport, session_id, message, context)
      end
    else
      {:error, :invalid_json} ->
        send_jsonrpc_error(conn, Error.protocol(:parse_error, %{message: "Invalid JSON"}), nil)

      {:error, reason} ->
        send_jsonrpc_error(conn, Error.protocol(:parse_error, %{reason: reason}), nil)
    end
  end

  defp handle_request(conn, transport, session_id, message, context, session_header) do
    case StreamableHTTP.handle_message(transport, session_id, message, context) do
      {:ok, response} ->
        conn
        |> put_resp_content_type("application/json")
        |> maybe_add_session_header(session_header, session_id)
        |> send_resp(200, response)

      {:error, %Error{} = error} ->
        send_jsonrpc_error(conn, error, message["id"])

      {:error, reason} ->
        send_jsonrpc_error(
          conn,
          Error.protocol(:internal_error, %{reason: reason}),
          message["id"]
        )
    end
  end

  defp handle_notification(conn, transport, session_id, message, context) do
    case StreamableHTTP.handle_message(transport, session_id, message, context) do
      {:ok, _} ->
        conn
        |> put_resp_content_type("application/json")
        |> send_resp(202, "{}")

      {:error, %Error{} = error} ->
        send_jsonrpc_error(conn, error, nil)

      {:error, reason} ->
        send_jsonrpc_error(conn, Error.protocol(:internal_error, %{reason: reason}), nil)
    end
  end

  defp maybe_read_body(%{body_params: %Unfetched{}} = conn, timeout) do
    case read_body(conn, read_timeout: timeout) do
      {:ok, body, conn} -> {:ok, body, conn}
      {:error, reason} -> {:error, reason}
    end
  end

  defp maybe_read_body(%{body_params: body} = conn, _timeout), do: {:ok, body, conn}

  defp parse_message(body) when is_binary(body) do
    case Message.decode(body) do
      {:ok, messages} -> {:ok, messages}
      {:error, _reason} -> {:error, :invalid_json}
    end
  end

  defp parse_message(body) when is_map(body) do
    case Message.validate_message(body) do
      {:ok, message} -> {:ok, [message]}
      {:error, _} -> {:error, :invalid_json}
    end
  end

  defp get_or_create_session_id(conn, session_header) do
    case get_req_header(conn, session_header) do
      [session_id] when is_binary(session_id) and session_id != "" -> session_id
      _ -> ID.generate_session_id()
    end
  end

  defp maybe_add_session_header(conn, session_header, session_id) do
    if get_req_header(conn, session_header) == [] do
      put_resp_header(conn, session_header, session_id)
    else
      conn
    end
  end

  defp send_jsonrpc_error(conn, %Error{} = error, id) do
    error_id = id || ID.generate_error_id()
    {:ok, encoded_error} = Error.to_json_rpc(error, error_id)

    conn
    |> put_resp_content_type("application/json")
    |> send_resp(400, encoded_error)
  end

  defp build_request_context(conn) do
    %{
      assigns: conn.assigns,
      type: :http,
      req_headers: conn.req_headers,
      query_params: fetch_query_params_safe(conn),
      remote_ip: conn.remote_ip,
      scheme: conn.scheme,
      host: conn.host,
      port: conn.port,
      request_path: conn.request_path
    }
  end

  defp fetch_query_params_safe(conn) do
    case conn.query_params do
      %Unfetched{} -> nil
      params -> params
    end
  end
end

    case conn.query_params do
      %Unfetched{} -> nil
      params -> params
    end
  end
end

defmodule TuistWeb.Plugs.MCPJsonResponsePlug do
  @moduledoc """
  A plug that wraps the Hermes StreamableHTTP plug and forces JSON responses
  instead of SSE streams for POST requests.

  The MCP spec allows servers to respond to POST requests with either
  `application/json` or `text/event-stream`. The Hermes plug defaults to SSE
  when the client's Accept header includes `text/event-stream`, which opens a
  streaming loop that never closes for simple request/response tool calls.

  This plug rewrites the Accept header on POST requests to remove
  `text/event-stream`, forcing the Hermes plug down the plain JSON response
  path. The original Accept header is preserved so that the Hermes validation
  still sees the required content types in the request context.
  """

  @behaviour Plug

  alias Hermes.Server.Transport.StreamableHTTP.Plug, as: HermesPlug

  @impl Plug
  def init(opts), do: HermesPlug.init(opts)

  @impl Plug
  def call(%Plug.Conn{method: "POST"} = conn, opts) do
    conn
    |> force_json_accept()
    |> HermesPlug.call(opts)
  end

  def call(conn, opts), do: HermesPlug.call(conn, opts)

  defp force_json_accept(conn) do
    %{conn | req_headers: replace_accept_header(conn.req_headers)}
  end

  defp replace_accept_header(headers) do
    Enum.map(headers, fn
      {"accept", _value} -> {"accept", "application/json"}
      other -> other
    end)
  end
end
